questions.question: Why does Temporal recommend that Activity functions be idempotent?

choices.text: So that the Temporal Service can compress the Event History more efficiently
Because Activities may be retried automatically, and a non-idempotent Activity could cause unintended side effects like duplicate charges
Because idempotent Activities are always faster and use less memory on the Worker
So that multiple Workflows can share the same Activity Definition without conflicts

questions.explanation: Temporal automatically retries failed Activities. If an Activity is not idempotent — meaning running it multiple times produces different results — you could end up with problems like charging a customer twice. Idempotency ensures that even if an Activity runs more than once, the final outcome is the same as if it ran just once. This is one of the core ways Temporal's durable execution model keeps your business logic safe.

questions.question: What is the main difference between an Activity Definition and an Activity Execution?

choices.text: An Activity Definition is the code specifying what the Activity does, while an Activity Execution is the actual running instance of that code
An Activity Definition runs on the Temporal Service, while an Activity Execution runs inside the Workflow
An Activity Definition is created at runtime, while an Activity Execution is written in advance by the developer
An Activity Definition handles retries automatically, while an Activity Execution must manage retries in application code

questions.explanation: Think of an Activity Definition as the recipe and an Activity Execution as the act of cooking. The Definition is the code you write that describes what the Activity should do. The Execution is what happens when Temporal actually runs that code on a Worker. A single Definition can produce many Executions over the lifetime of your application.

questions.question: When an Activity fails and is retried, where does the retry start by default?

choices.text: From the last line of code that ran successfully inside the Activity function
From the step after the one that failed, using state saved in the Event History
From the very beginning of the Activity function, in its initial state
From a checkpoint automatically created by the Temporal Service mid-execution

questions.explanation: Unlike a Workflow, which replays its history to restore state, an Activity always restarts from scratch when retried. The only way to resume mid-Activity is to use Heartbeat details — a feature that lets you store checkpoint data on the Temporal Service so a retry can pick up where the previous attempt left off. This is why breaking large tasks into smaller Activities is a recommended best practice.

questions.question: What is Asynchronous Activity Completion useful for?

choices.text: Running multiple Activities in parallel inside a single Workflow Execution
Letting an Activity function return early while an external system later reports the final result
Skipping the retry policy when an Activity completes faster than expected
Allowing the Workflow to modify Activity parameters after the Activity has started

questions.explanation: Asynchronous Activity Completion lets an Activity function exit before the work is actually done. The real result is delivered later — by an external system, a webhook, or a human — using a Task Token or Activity ID to identify which execution to complete. This is handy when work is handed off to something outside your control, like a third-party API or a human approval step that could take days.

questions.question: What happens to the parent Workflow when one of its Activities is Paused using an Activity Operation?

choices.text: The Workflow is also paused automatically and stops processing Signals and Queries
The Workflow is terminated with an error until the Activity is Unpaused
The Workflow continues running normally and can still receive Signals, Queries, and Updates
The Workflow waits for the Activity to be Unpaused before it can process any new tasks

questions.explanation: Pausing an Activity only stops the Temporal Service from scheduling new retries of that specific Activity. The parent Workflow keeps running — it can still receive Signals, respond to Queries, and process Updates. This is useful when an external dependency is down and you want to hold retries without freezing everything else. Note that the Workflow code itself has no visibility into the Pause; it does not appear in the Event History.

questions.question: You need to call a payment API from a Temporal Activity. The payment provider supports idempotency keys. What is the recommended approach to safely handle automatic retries?

choices.text: Disable retries on the Activity entirely so the payment API is called exactly once
Use a combination of the Workflow Run ID and Activity ID as an idempotency key when calling the payment API
Wrap the payment call in a try-catch block and manually record whether the charge succeeded in a local variable
Split the Activity into a read step and a write step, and only apply retries to the read step

questions.explanation: The Workflow Run ID and Activity ID together form a stable, unique identifier that stays the same across retry attempts of the same Activity Execution. Passing this as an idempotency key to the payment provider lets the provider detect duplicate requests and ignore them, preventing double charges. Local variables won't survive a Worker crash, and disabling retries removes one of Temporal's key durability benefits.

questions.question: An Activity completes successfully, but the Worker crashes before it can report the result to the Temporal Service. What happens next?

choices.text: Temporal detects the Worker crash immediately and marks the Activity as failed in the Event History.
The Activity result is buffered on the Worker and replayed once the Worker restarts and reconnects.
Temporal relies on the Start-To-Close Timeout to detect the missing report and retries the Activity according to its Retry Policy.
The Workflow is automatically cancelled because the Event History can no longer be trusted after a Worker crash.

questions.explanation: Temporal has no direct mechanism to detect a Worker crash. Instead, it relies on the Start-To-Close Timeout: if the Activity Task doesn't report completion within that window, Temporal assumes something went wrong and retries the Activity. This is a core reason why Activities should be idempotent — the Activity function may execute successfully more than once (e.g., a payment charge), and without idempotency that can cause incorrect side effects. Option A is wrong because Temporal cannot directly observe Worker crashes. Option B is wrong — there is no result buffering on the Worker side. Option D is wrong; the Workflow continues running and the Activity is simply retried.

questions.question: You want to construct an idempotency key for an Activity that calls an external payment API. Which combination does Temporal recommend?

choices.text: The current timestamp combined with the Worker host name, since these are unique per execution attempt.
A random UUID generated at the start of the Activity function, regenerated on each retry attempt.
The Workflow Run ID combined with the Activity ID, because this is consistent across retry attempts but unique across Workflow Executions.
The Workflow ID alone, because it is globally unique and never changes across the lifetime of the Workflow.

questions.explanation: Temporal explicitly recommends using the Workflow Run ID + Activity ID as the idempotency key when calling external services from an Activity. This combination stays the same across retry attempts of the same Activity Execution (so duplicates are detected), but differs across distinct Workflow Executions (so different workflows don't collide). Option A fails because timestamps and host names change between retries. Option B fails for the same reason — a new UUID per retry defeats the purpose of an idempotency key. Option D fails because the Workflow ID alone is reused across multiple Runs (Continue-As-New or re-runs), so it is not unique enough to distinguish individual Activity Executions.

questions.question: When should you use Asynchronous Activity Completion instead of having the Workflow wait for a Signal from an external system?

choices.text: When the external system is fully reliable and can always be trusted to send a callback, and the Activity does not need to heartbeat or receive cancellations.
When you want shorter retry cycles on failure, since async completion allows the Activity to finish quickly and be retried with a short Start-To-Close Timeout.
When the external system is unreliable and might fail to signal, or when the external process needs to heartbeat or receive cancellation from the Workflow.
When the result will be available within seconds, because async completion avoids the overhead of keeping the Workflow blocked on a long-running Activity.

questions.explanation: Asynchronous Activity Completion is the right choice when the external system may fail to send a callback (Signal), or when the external process needs to heartbeat to show liveness or receive cancellation requests from the Workflow. If the external system is reliable and doesn't need those features, using a Signal is often simpler and allows you to set a shorter Start-To-Close Timeout on the Activity (e.g., 1 minute to notify the system), so failures are detected and retried faster rather than waiting for a long timeout. Option A describes the scenario where Signals are actually preferable, not async completion. Option B is backwards — Signals give you the shorter retry cycle, not async completion. Option D is a misunderstanding; async completion is designed for long-running external work, not quick results.

questions.question: You break a three-step Activity (DB lookup → microservice call → filesystem write) into three separate Activities instead of one. What is the primary trade-off you are accepting?

choices.text: Each Activity now runs on a separate Worker, which increases network latency and reduces overall throughput significantly.
The Workflow loses the ability to retry individual steps independently because Temporal only supports retry policies at the Workflow level, not per Activity.
The Workflow's Event History grows larger because there are now three Activity Executions instead of one, each producing its own set of Events.
The individual Activities can no longer share context from previous steps, since Temporal does not allow passing output from one Activity as input to the next.

questions.explanation: Splitting a multi-step Activity into several smaller Activities improves idempotency and failure recovery — only the failed step is retried instead of the entire sequence. The trade-off is a larger Event History, because each Activity Execution produces its own events (Scheduled, Started, Completed, etc.). Option A is wrong — Activities on the same Task Queue are dispatched to available Workers and don't require separate Workers. Option B is wrong — Temporal absolutely supports per-Activity Retry Policies, and that's precisely the benefit of splitting. Option D is wrong — the Workflow code can freely pass return values from one Activity as arguments to the next.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the scope of an Activity Type in Temporal?

choices.text: Activity Types are globally unique within a Namespace, so two different Task Queues cannot register an Activity with the same name.
Activity Types are scoped to a Task Queue, so different Task Queues can have Activities with the same name that map to different implementations.
Activity Types are scoped to a single Worker process, meaning the same Activity name on two different Workers in the same Task Queue refers to separate definitions.
Activity Types are scoped to a Workflow Execution, so an Activity name is only valid for the duration of the Workflow that first registered it.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly states that Activity Types are scoped through Task Queues. This means the same Activity Type name can exist on multiple Task Queues referring to completely different implementations, without collision. This is important in practice when you have multiple microservices or deployment environments each registering their own Activities under the same logical name. Option A is wrong — there is no Namespace-level global uniqueness enforced for Activity Type names. Option C is wrong — the scoping is at the Task Queue level, not the Worker process level. Option D is wrong — Activity Types are not tied to a single Workflow Execution.

questions.question: A paused Activity has a Schedule-To-Close Timeout of 30 minutes and has already been waiting paused for 25 minutes. What happens when the timeout elapses?

choices.text: The Schedule-To-Close Timeout is automatically extended when an Activity is paused, so it won't expire until the Activity is unpaused and given a chance to run.
Pausing an Activity resets the Schedule-To-Close Timeout clock, so the 30-minute window starts over from the moment the Activity is unpaused.
The Activity can still time out while paused; the Schedule-To-Close Timeout continues to count down and is not affected by the Pause. Use update-options to extend it if needed.
The Activity transitions to a terminal failed state because paused Activities are not eligible for retry, making the timeout behave like a non-retryable error.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly warns: 'A Paused Activity can still time out. Pause doesn't stop or extend the Schedule-To-Close Timeout.' The timeout clock keeps running regardless of the pause state. If you need the Activity to stay paused beyond its current timeout window, you must use `temporal activity update-options` to extend the Schedule-To-Close Timeout before it elapses. Option A is wrong — there is no automatic extension on pause. Option B is wrong — the clock is not reset. Option D is wrong — the behavior is a standard timeout, not a non-retryable failure; the distinction about retries not being scheduled is a separate effect of the pause itself.

questions.question: An Activity function returns without the Activity Execution completing, and a remote system later calls the Temporal Client to complete it. What Temporal feature makes this possible, and what are the two ways to identify the correct execution?

choices.text: Workflow Signals — the remote system sends a Signal carrying the result, identified by the Workflow ID and an optional Run ID.
Asynchronous Activity Completion — the remote system calls the Temporal Client using either a Task Token or the combination of Activity ID, Workflow ID, and optionally Run ID.
Asynchronous Activity Completion — the remote system must use a Task Token, because Activity ID alone is not guaranteed to be unique across retry attempts.
Standalone Activity — the SDK Client invokes the Activity directly and holds an open connection, completing it when the remote system sends back the result.

questions.explanation: Asynchronous Activity Completion is the feature that allows an Activity function to return without marking the Activity Execution as complete. The Temporal Client can then be used from anywhere — including a remote system — to complete the Activity. The client accepts either a Task Token (unique per Activity Task Execution) or the combination of Activity ID + Workflow ID (+ optionally Run ID). Option A describes Signals, which are a different mechanism used to send data to the Workflow, not to complete an Activity. Option C is wrong because the docs explicitly state that Activity ID + Workflow ID is a valid alternative to Task Token, and actually preferred in some cases since Task Tokens can become invalid after a retry. Option D describes Standalone Activities, which are a different concept entirely.

questions.question: Your payment processing Activity is stuck in exponential backoff after a transient downstream failure. The on-call engineer uses `temporal activity pause` to stop retries while the downstream service recovers. Fifteen minutes later, the downstream service is healthy. Which of the following correctly describes what the engineer must do next, and why?

choices.text: Nothing — once the downstream service recovers, Temporal automatically detects the pause condition is resolved and schedules the next retry without operator intervention.
Restart the Worker process — paused Activities are held in Worker memory, so recycling the Worker releases the pause and triggers an immediate retry attempt.
Cancel and restart the Workflow — pausing an Activity blocks the parent Workflow from making progress, so the only recovery path is to terminate and re-run the Workflow.

Run `temporal activity unpause` targeting the same workflow ID and activity ID — pauses are enforced server-side and persist until explicitly unpaused, at which point the next execution starts immediately.

questions.explanation: Activity Pause is enforced server-side, not by the SDK or Worker. The Temporal Service stops scheduling retries and holds that state indefinitely until an operator explicitly calls `temporal activity unpause`. Temporal has no mechanism to auto-detect that an external dependency has recovered. Workers have no knowledge of the pause state — they simply receive (or don't receive) new Activity Tasks. Crucially, pausing an Activity does NOT block the parent Workflow; Signals, Queries, and Updates continue to work normally. So restarting the Workflow is both unnecessary and destructive. The correct operational path is always a deliberate `unpause` command.

questions.question: A Worker executing a long-running Activity crashes just after the Activity function completes its business logic but before it can report success to the Temporal Service. No heartbeating was configured. Looking at the Temporal Web UI, the Activity shows as still Running and eventually retries. Your payment Activity is NOT idempotent. What is the most accurate description of the risk here?

choices.text: There is no risk — Temporal's exactly-once delivery guarantee ensures the ActivityTaskCompleted event was already written to the Event History before the Worker could process the result, preventing a second charge.
The Activity will not retry because Temporal detected the Worker crash via its health check, and the platform marks the Activity as failed rather than retrying it.
The retry will only re-execute steps after the last checkpoint, so as long as the payment call itself succeeded, the retry skips that step and only re-executes subsequent logic.

The retry will re-execute the entire Activity function from the beginning, potentially issuing a duplicate payment charge, because Temporal relies on the Start-To-Close timeout to detect task loss rather than detecting the crash directly.

questions.explanation: Temporal does not detect Worker crashes directly. It relies on the Start-To-Close timeout: if the Worker doesn't report back within that window, Temporal assumes the task is lost and schedules a retry. Because Activities restart from their initial state on retry (unless heartbeat details are used for checkpointing), the entire function body — including the payment call — runs again. The EventHistory only records an ActivityTaskCompleted event when the Worker successfully reports back, so a crash before that report leaves no trace of the successful execution. This is precisely why Temporal strongly recommends that Activities performing writes be idempotent, and why idempotency keys (combining Workflow Run ID + Activity ID) are the recommended pattern for payment systems.

questions.question: An engineer is designing an Activity that calls a human-review external system that can take up to five days to respond. The engineer is debating between Asynchronous Activity Completion and using a Signal. The external system is fully under their control. A key concern is: if the Activity fails to notify the external system (e.g., the Worker dies mid-execution without throwing an error), how quickly will the failure be retried? Which design minimizes retry latency in this failure scenario?

choices.text: Asynchronous Activity Completion, because the Task Token allows the external system to directly notify Temporal of the failure, triggering an immediate retry without waiting for any timeout.
Asynchronous Activity Completion with a short heartbeat interval, because heartbeating will detect the silent Worker crash and retry the notification step within the heartbeat timeout window.

Using a Signal, with a short Start-To-Close timeout on the Activity (e.g., one minute). The Activity's job is only to notify the external system; if it silently fails, it is retried within a minute rather than waiting five days.
Both approaches have identical retry latency for silent failures because Temporal uses the same internal timeout mechanism regardless of whether you use Async Completion or Signals.

questions.explanation: With Async Completion, you must set the Start-To-Close timeout to match the maximum wait time (five days), because the Activity is considered open until the external system completes it. If the Worker silently dies before notifying the external system, the failure won't be retried for five days — when that timeout fires. With the Signal approach, the Activity's only job is to send the notification, so you can set a very short timeout (e.g., one minute). If the notification step silently fails, the retry fires within a minute. The external system then Signals the Workflow when the human responds. This decoupling of 'notify' from 'wait for response' is the key architectural insight. Heartbeating doesn't help with silent failures in the Async Completion case because the Activity function has already returned (that's the point of async completion).

questions.question: You have an Activity that performs three sequential steps: (1) fetch config from a database, (2) call a downstream microservice with that config, (3) write the result to a file. Step 3 fails with a permissions error. On retry, all three steps execute again. A colleague suggests splitting this into three separate Activities to improve idempotency. What is the accurate trade-off the team must accept if they follow this advice?

choices.text: Splitting into three Activities eliminates idempotency concerns entirely, because completed Activities are guaranteed to never re-execute, even if the Workflow is replayed after a code deploy.

The Event History will grow larger because each Activity Execution generates its own history events (scheduled, started, completed), tripling the number of Activity-related events compared to a single Activity.
Three Activities will cause three separate Task Queue polls, which substantially increases Temporal Service load and is generally discouraged for activities that complete in under a second.
Splitting Activities reduces retry granularity control, because Temporal applies the same Retry Policy to all Activities in a Workflow and you cannot configure independent timeouts per Activity.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly calls out this trade-off: splitting into more granular Activities improves idempotency recovery (only the failed step retries) but comes at the cost of a larger Event History, since each Activity Execution generates multiple events (ActivityTaskScheduled, ActivityTaskStarted, ActivityTaskCompleted, etc.). This is a real operational consideration — Event History size affects replay time, storage cost, and eventually the need for Continue-As-New. The other options are incorrect: completed Activities do not re-execute during replay, but that's not what this question is about (the failure is before completion). Retry Policies are configurable independently per Activity. And Task Queue poll overhead is not a documented concern for short activities in this context.

questions.question: An Activity is using Asynchronous Completion. The Activity function runs, passes its Task Token to an external service, and then the Activity fails before returning the 'I am completing asynchronously' marker to the SDK. Later, when the external service tries to complete the Activity using the original Task Token, the call fails. What is the root cause, and what is the recommended mitigation?

choices.text: The Task Token expired due to the Start-To-Close timeout, so the external service should re-fetch a fresh token by querying the Workflow's Event History through the Temporal API.

The Activity failure caused Temporal to invalidate the original Task Token because a new Task Execution was started on retry, making the original token stale. Using Activity ID and Workflow ID instead of a Task Token avoids this problem.
Task Tokens are only valid within the same Worker process that generated them. Since the Activity failed, the Worker discarded the token from memory, which is why the external service can't use it.
Asynchronous Completion cannot be combined with retries. Once an Activity fails, the async completion path is permanently closed and you must restart the Workflow to re-issue the external call.

questions.explanation: A Task Token uniquely identifies a specific Activity Task Execution, not the Activity Execution as a whole. When the Activity fails before returning the async marker, Temporal retries it, creating a new Task Execution with a new Task Token. The external service still holds the old Task Token, which is now invalid — it references an execution that no longer exists. The documentation explicitly calls this out and recommends passing the Activity ID and Workflow ID (optionally Run ID) to the external system instead. These identifiers remain stable across retry attempts, so the external service can always complete the correct Activity Execution regardless of how many retries occurred before a successful async pickup.

questions.question: Your team notices that a long-running Activity is stuck retrying for hours without making progress. An operator runs `temporal activity pause` targeting the Activity. The Activity currently has no heartbeating configured. What happens to the currently in-flight execution of the Activity at the moment the pause command is issued?

choices.text: The in-flight execution is immediately terminated by the Temporal Service sending a cancellation signal to the Worker, regardless of heartbeat configuration.
The in-flight execution is unaffected and continues running to completion. If it succeeds, the result is delivered to the Workflow normally. If it fails, no retry is scheduled after it ends.
The Temporal Service marks the Activity as paused and also cancels the Schedule-To-Close timeout, so the Activity can safely run to completion without risk of timing out during the paused window.
The Worker detects the pause via its next long-poll and raises a pause exception in the Activity function, allowing the Activity code to perform cleanup before stopping.

questions.explanation: Whether a Pause interrupts an in-flight Activity execution depends entirely on whether the Activity is heartbeating. For Activities without heartbeating, the Temporal Service has no channel to communicate the pause to the running Activity — there is no heartbeat response to intercept. The Activity continues running until it naturally completes or fails. If it succeeds, the result is delivered to the Workflow as normal. If it fails, the pause takes effect and no retry is scheduled. Critically, the Schedule-To-Close timeout is NOT paused or extended — this is an important gotcha noted in the documentation. Only Activities with active heartbeating receive a pause-specific interruption on their next heartbeat call.

questions.question: A team adds internal retry logic (a for-loop with try/catch) inside an Activity function to handle transient errors from a flaky API. Three months later, the on-call team is struggling to debug why some Activities appear healthy in Temporal UI but the flaky API is actually failing hundreds of times. What is the operational problem with this pattern?

choices.text: Internal retries inside an Activity cause Temporal to lose track of the Activity's progress, eventually triggering a Schedule-To-Close timeout even when the Activity is actually making progress.
Temporal's replay mechanism re-executes internal retry loops on Workflow replay, causing the Activity to run significantly more iterations during replay than during original execution.

Internal retries prevent Temporal from recording intermediate failures as Activity retry attempts, hiding failure frequency from Temporal UI metrics and making it harder to observe and debug problems in production.

Internal retries bypass the exponential backoff enforced by the Temporal Retry Policy, which causes excessive load on the flaky downstream API and may result in the Worker being rate-limited by the Temporal Service.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly warns against internal retries for exactly this reason: they prevent users from counting failure metrics and make it harder to debug in Temporal UI when something is wrong. When failures are swallowed inside a for-loop, Temporal only sees a single, long-running Activity Task Execution — no failed attempts are recorded, no retry events appear in the Event History, and observability tools like Grafana dashboards that track activity failure rates show nothing. This masks real problems. The recommended approach is to let Temporal's built-in retry policy handle transient failures, which records each attempt in a visible and observable way. Additionally, internal retries can artificially lengthen the required Activity timeout.

questions.question: A payment processing Activity uses asynchronous completion: it records a task token, fires a request to an external payment processor, and returns `ErrResultPending`. The external processor occasionally fails to call back, so the team sets a `StartToCloseTimeout` of 7 days to give it time. In a post-incident review, the team discovers that when the payment processor fails silently on step 2 (the outbound call crashes without throwing), the Activity isn't retried for up to 7 days. Which redesign most directly eliminates this silent-failure window while preserving the asynchronous completion model?

choices.text: Switch from task-token-based async completion to Activity-ID-based async completion, because Activity IDs survive retries while task tokens become invalid after a failure, allowing the payment processor to complete a later retry attempt.

Keep async completion for the final result but move the outbound call into a short-timeout Activity (e.g., 1 minute). Complete that Activity normally after the call succeeds, then wait for the processor's callback via a Signal to the Workflow, so silent failures in the outbound call are retried quickly.

Enable Heartbeating inside the async-completion Activity so that the Temporal Service detects Worker crashes via the Heartbeat timeout, triggering a retry well before the 7-day StartToCloseTimeout expires.

Set a ScheduleToClose timeout of 1 hour instead of 7 days, and configure the Retry Policy with a maximum of 10 attempts, so that a silent failure in step 2 causes a retry within hours rather than days.

questions.explanation: The core problem is that the outbound call (step 2) can fail silently—no error is thrown, so the Activity neither completes nor fails, and Temporal only detects the stall via StartToCloseTimeout. Async completion is designed for scenarios where an *external system* provides the final result; it intentionally removes the timeout pressure from the activity while it waits. The fix is to separate concerns: use a short-timeout Activity solely to trigger the external call (fast retries on silent failure), then let the Workflow wait indefinitely via a Signal for the callback. Option A is a real concern—task tokens *do* become invalid across retries—but switching to Activity-ID-based completion doesn't address the 7-day stall. Option C seems plausible, but heartbeating requires the Activity to be actively running and calling heartbeat; a Worker crash or silent network failure before the heartbeat is sent still leaves the Activity orphaned until the heartbeat timeout fires (which is itself bounded by StartToCloseTimeout). Option D just shortens the window; it doesn't eliminate the class of problem. The deeper design principle: async completion trades retry agility for external-system integration. When reliability of the *trigger* step matters, decouple it into a separate Activity with a short timeout, and use Signals for the callback. This is the exact trade-off the documentation describes in the 'When to use Async Completion' section.

questions.question: An Activity is paused via `temporal activity pause` because a downstream API is degraded. The Activity is long-running and uses Heartbeat with a 30-second HeartbeatTimeout. The team has also set a ScheduleToCloseTimeout of 2 hours on the Activity. After pausing, the on-call engineer waits 90 minutes investigating the downstream issue before running `temporal activity unpause`. What is the actual runtime behavior during those 90 minutes, and what risk does the engineer face when they unpause?

choices.text: Because Pause intercepts Heartbeats server-side, the HeartbeatTimeout clock is suspended while the Activity is paused. The ScheduleToCloseTimeout continues running, so unpausing after 90 minutes is safe as long as less than 2 hours have elapsed since the Activity was originally scheduled.

The in-flight execution is interrupted on the next Heartbeat, which fires within 30 seconds. No new retries are scheduled. The ScheduleToCloseTimeout continues counting down during the pause. If 2 hours elapse before unpause, the Activity times out permanently and cannot be recovered by unpausing.

Pause stops the ScheduleToCloseTimeout clock along with retries, so the full 2-hour window is available after unpause. The in-flight execution is interrupted on the next Heartbeat, but the timeout budget is effectively frozen until the operator unpauses.

The Activity ignores the Pause signal because it is mid-execution; the Pause takes effect only after the current execution completes. The ScheduleToCloseTimeout continues, and if it expires before the execution finishes, the Activity fails non-retryably regardless of the Pause state.

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly states: 'A Paused Activity can still time out. Pause doesn't stop or extend the ScheduleToClose Timeout.' This is the critical operator trap. The ScheduleToCloseTimeout is an absolute wall-clock deadline measured from when the Activity was first scheduled—it is unaffected by Pause, Unpause, or any operational control. Meanwhile, because this Activity uses Heartbeat, the in-flight execution *is* interrupted on the next heartbeat (within 30 seconds), and a Pause-specific error is raised. No further retries are scheduled. So during those 90 minutes, the Activity is interrupted and idle, but the 2-hour countdown is still running. If the operator waits longer than the remaining ScheduleToCloseTimeout, the Activity will time out and that timeout is terminal—it cannot be recovered by unpausing. Option C describes a 'frozen clock' behavior that doesn't exist. Option A's claim that HeartbeatTimeout is suspended is also incorrect. Option D is wrong about when Pause takes effect for heartbeating Activities. The production lesson: before pausing a long-running Activity for investigation, use `temporal activity update-options` to extend the ScheduleToCloseTimeout *first*, or be very aware of the remaining budget. Pause is an operational tool, not a timeout shield.

choices.text: There is no risk because completed Activities are never re-executed during replay, regardless of whether the execution is a continuation or a fresh run. The payment service will never see duplicate calls with this key format.

The RunID changes with each ContinueAsNew, so the idempotency key changes across continuations for the same logical Activity execution. A charge that succeeded in Run 1 could be attempted again in Run 2 with a different key, resulting in a duplicate charge that the payment service cannot deduplicate.

The ActivityID is reset to a default value after ContinueAsNew, making the combined key non-unique across continuations even though the RunID changes, which causes key collisions rather than duplicate charges.

The risk only exists if the Retry Policy allows more than one attempt, because a single-attempt Activity that completed in the previous run cannot be scheduled again in the new run after ContinueAsNew.

questions.explanation: The documentation specifically warns: 'You can use a combination of the Workflow Run ID and the Activity ID as an idempotency key since this is guaranteed to be consistent across retry attempts but unique among Workflow Executions.' The key insight is the phrase 'unique among Workflow Executions'—a ContinueAsNew creates a *new* Run ID. If the workflow logic re-schedules the same logical payment activity in the new run (e.g., because it needs to retry something from the previous run's window, or because carry-over state causes re-execution), the idempotency key will differ from the one used in the prior run. The payment service sees a brand-new key and processes a duplicate charge. The correct idempotency key for cross-continuation safety should be derived from the WorkflowID (stable across ContinueAsNew) plus a business-level identifier (e.g., order ID), not the RunID. Option A is wrong—while completed activities in the *same* run are not replayed, the new run after ContinueAsNew starts fresh and can re-schedule activities. Option C invents non-existent behavior around ActivityID resets. Option D confuses retry policy with the ContinueAsNew execution boundary. The production insight: RunID-based idempotency keys are safe within a run but break across continuations. For payment systems with ContinueAsNew, use WorkflowID + a business transaction ID as the idempotency key.

questions.question: A TypeScript worker runs a file-transcoding Activity with no Heartbeat configured. The Activity typically takes 45 minutes. An operator uses `temporal activity pause` to hold the Activity while a storage migration is in progress. Meanwhile, the Worker process is gracefully shut down for a deployment. After the deployment, the operator runs `temporal activity unpause`. What is the actual sequence of events the operator should expect?

choices.text: The Pause immediately stops the in-flight execution because Worker shutdown sends a cancellation signal to all running Activities. After unpause, the Activity resumes from where it left off using the last recorded Heartbeat details, which in this case are empty, so it restarts from the beginning.

Because the Activity has no Heartbeat, Pause cannot interrupt the in-flight execution—it continues running on the original Worker. The Worker shutdown then terminates the execution mid-flight. Since Pause is active, no retry is scheduled after the Worker dies, leaving the Activity stuck in a paused-failed state until the operator also runs unpause.

The Pause takes effect after the in-flight execution ends (no Heartbeat means no interruption). Worker shutdown terminates the execution. No retry is scheduled because Pause is active. After unpause, the Temporal Service schedules a new execution immediately, and it starts from the beginning since there are no Heartbeat details.

Worker shutdown triggers an automatic Unpause for all paused Activities on that Worker before it terminates, so the Activity enters the normal retry queue. The operator's subsequent unpause command is a no-op since the Activity has already been retried by the time it runs.

questions.explanation: This question requires chaining three documented behaviors together. First: 'Activities without Heartbeat continue running to completion. If the execution succeeds, the result is delivered to the Workflow normally. If it fails, no retry is scheduled. Pause takes effect after the in-flight execution ends.' So Pause does NOT interrupt a non-heartbeating Activity immediately. Second: the Worker shuts down, killing the in-flight execution mid-flight—this counts as a failure. Third: because Pause is active server-side, the Temporal Service does not schedule a retry even though the execution failed. The Activity is now in a paused state with a failed execution and no pending retry. Fourth: the operator runs unpause, which causes 'the next execution [to start] immediately'—a fresh execution from the beginning, since there are no Heartbeat details to resume from. Option A incorrectly claims the Activity resumes from Heartbeat details (there are none) and incorrectly states that Worker shutdown sends cancellation signals that stop the Activity mid-execution before Pause takes effect. Option B correctly identifies that no retry is scheduled, but incorrectly says the Activity is stuck in a 'paused-failed state' requiring separate action—the unpause is what resolves it. Option D invents a Worker-shutdown auto-unpause behavior that doesn't exist. The production takeaway: for long-running Activities in environments with rolling deployments, the lack of Heartbeat combined with Pause creates a gap where Worker restarts silently lose in-flight work without automatic recovery. Always add Heartbeating to Activities longer than a deploy window.
